title: SEGMENT 5 — Warfarin: the original pharmacogenomics poster child

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Warfarin: the original pharmacogenomics poster child
Now let's make all of this concrete.
The drug we're going to use to learn the field's methodology is warfarin.
The same warfarin from the opening scene in Segment 1.
The most-studied, most-litigated, most-clinically-painful pharmacogenomics target in the history of the field.
The drug that taught the world what variant-driven dosing actually looks like, and also the drug that taught the world how hard it is to actually implement pharmacogenomics in the clinic.
Both lessons matter.
Warfarin is a blood thinner.
Its job is to prevent or treat blood clots.
The mechanism is beautiful.
Your body needs vitamin K to make certain clotting factors — proteins in your blood that, when activated in a cascade, form clots.
Warfarin blocks an enzyme called VKORC1 — vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1 — that recycles used vitamin K back into the active form.
Block VKORC1, and you starve the clotting factor production line of vitamin K, and clotting slows down.
It's also a phenomenally dangerous drug, because the window between effective and lethal is small.
Too little warfarin: you don't prevent the clot, the patient throws a clot to their lung or their brain.
Too much warfarin: you cause a bleed — a brain hemorrhage, a GI bleed, an intracranial bleed during a minor head injury.
Warfarin is, by some estimates, the drug responsible for more emergency-room visits per prescription than almost any other drug on the market.
The standard practice is to monitor a blood test called the INR every few weeks for the rest of the patient's life and titrate the dose endlessly.
Here's the genetic part.
The dose of warfarin a particular patient needs, to land in their therapeutic window, varies fivefold across the population.
Some patients need half a milligram a day.
Some patients need ten milligrams a day.
Same blood thinning effect.
Vastly different doses.
About forty percent of that variability is explained by two genes.
CYP2C9.
The enzyme that metabolizes warfarin, breaking it down so the body can clear it.
The two major non-functional variants are CYP2C9\2 and CYP2C9\3.

Patients with these variants metabolize warfarin more slowly than normal — their slow chef can't break down the warfarin at standard rates, so the warfarin builds up, and they need a lower dose to achieve the same blood-thinning effect.
A \3/\3 patient may need a third of the standard dose.
VKORC1.
The target of warfarin itself.
There's a common variant in the promoter region of VKORC1 — a single base change — that reduces the amount of VKORC1 enzyme the cell makes.
Less enzyme means less warfarin is needed to block it.
Patients with this variant are warfarin-sensitive and need lower doses.
Now, the rest of the variability comes from non-genetic factors — age, weight, diet (vitamin K intake from leafy greens), other drugs, kidney and liver function.
Plus another genetic factor or two we now know matter, like CYP4F2 in some populations.
But CYP2C9 and VKORC1 are the big two, and together they're enough to make a substantial difference in initial dose prediction.
So in the early 2000s, when these associations were nailed down, the field got excited.
We can sequence two genes, plug the genotype into a dosing formula along with age, weight, and a few other variables, and predict the right starting dose for each patient.
We can stop the dangerous trial-and-error period.
We can prevent bleeds and clots in the first month of therapy.
And then, in a moment that I think every pharmacogenomics researcher remembers, came the disappointment.
A large clinical trial called COAG — Clarification of Optimal Anticoagulation through Genetics — was run in the early 2010s.
Patients about to start warfarin were randomized to two arms.
One arm got the standard clinical algorithm — dose by age, weight, comorbidities, the usual.
The other arm got the genetic algorithm — same clinical factors, plus CYP2C9 and VKORC1 genotyping, plugged into a formula.
The result: no significant difference in time spent in therapeutic INR range.
The genetic arm did not, in this trial, beat the clinical arm.
This was, depending on how you read it, either a catastrophe for pharmacogenomics or a lesson about how the field works.
There's a parallel trial called EU-PACT that ran in Europe at the same time and did find a benefit.
The trials differed in important ways — the patient populations, the control arm algorithm, the time frame.

The honest answer is that the warfarin pharmacogenomics story is complicated, and the marginal benefit of genotyping for warfarin specifically, in the era of the alternative drugs we now have, is real but modest.

But — and this is the part that matters for the rest of the lecture — by the time COAG and EU-PACT were running, the broader field had already shown that for other drug-gene pairs, the benefit was huge and unambiguous.
Clopidogrel, codeine, abacavir, carbamazepine, thiopurines, 5-FU.
Warfarin was the proof of concept that pharmacogenomics could work in principle.
The other drugs proved it works in practice.
There's a coda.
The newer anticoagulants — drugs like apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran — don't require monitoring and have much wider therapeutic windows.
In many patients they've replaced warfarin entirely.
So even where warfarin pharmacogenomics didn't dramatically reshape practice, the broader pharmaceutical pipeline gave us alternatives.
Sometimes the answer to a hard pharmacogenomics problem is "use a different drug.
" We'll come back to that.
So that's warfarin.
The original poster child.

CYP2C9 plus VKORC1, ~40% of dose variability, dosing nomograms still in use, modest benefit from genotyping demonstrated in some trials, the drug that taught the field both the promise and the difficulty of clinical implementation.
Carry it with you as the prototype.
Most of what follows is variations on this theme.
